anzsic_codes.name: Other Auxiliary Finance and Investment Services

merger_description:
Hillhouse Investment Management, Ltd (**Hillhouse**) is a global alternative investment management firm headquartered in Singapore which focuses on long-term investments in high quality businesses. It has offices in London, Sydney, Tokyo, Hong Kong, Beijing, Mumbai and New York.

The target, Barwon Investment Partners Pty Ltd (**Barwon**), is an Australian alternatives fund manager focusing on healthcare property, real estate credit and private equity. Barwon has one office in Sydney.

The parties supply investment management services, which involves the professional and customised management of various financial assets on behalf of institutional and wholesale investor clients.

Hillhouse proposes to acquire a majority interest in Barwon.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Barwon Bidco Pty Ltd (Bidco), which is ultimately owned by
Hillhouse Investment Management, Ltd (Hillhouse), applied for a
notification waiver in respect of its proposed acquisition of a
majority interest in Barwon Investment Partners Pty Ltd (Barwon), as
described in the transaction documents provided as part of the
application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, Hillhouse, is a global alternative investment manager
that deploys capital across multiple strategies, including public
equities, private equity, private credit, and real assets. Through its
private equity arm, Hillhouse invests in long-term global
opportunities across the business services, healthcare, consumer,
and industrials sectors.
The target, Barwon, is a specialist fund manager investing in
alternative assets across healthcare property, real estate credit and
global investments, as well as in private equity for institutional and
wholesale investors.
Both parties supply investment management services in Australia,
although their focus is on different asset classes and in different
sectors.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. there is no or limited horizontal overlap between Hillhouse
and Barwon’s respective services and investment portfolios
(including between their connected entities)
b. there are alternative suppliers of investment management
services in Australia
c. there appears to be a low risk of foreclosure or other
concerning vertical or conglomerate effects resulting from
exclusionary conduct post-acquisition.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.
